coredata.dc:title: A cucurbit[6]uril-based carbon dot for recognizing metal ions and anions in solutions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, XingYue
Yu, ZhiChao
Hu, JianHang
Xiao, Bo
Zhang, TingTing
Li, Kui
Chen, Chang
Tao, Zhu
Xiao, Xin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using cucurbit[6]uril (Q[6]) and mandelic acid (MA). Compared with other carbon quantum dots, cucurbit[6]uril has the advantage that its original rigid macrocyclic skeleton was completely retained during the synthesis process. In addition, the performance of the Q[6]-CQDs were characterized by fluorescence and NMR spectroscopies, then the crystal structure of Q[6]-MA-[CdCl4]2- was determined by the single crystal X-ray crystallography. The Q[6]-CQDs showed good water solubility and stable optical property. Subsequently, using the obtained Q[6]-CQDs, a universal fluorescent probe for detecting and recognizing Fe3+, Ba2+, Al3+, I- and ClO- has been developed based on macrocyclic chemistry. Under ideal conditions, the detection limits were calculated to be 3.89 × 10-6 M, 2.58 × 10-5 M, 1.42 × 10-5 M, 6.84 × 10-6 M and 1.50 × 10-5 M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cucurbit[6]uril
Mandelic acid
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of Q[6]-CQDs. Inset: Photographs of the solution of the Q[6]-CQDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (b) the fluorescence emission spectra of Q[6]-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 210 to 300 nm; (c) fluorescence responses of the Q[6]-CQDs in different pH solutions; (d)FIIR spectra of Q[6]-CQDs. (e) FTIR spectra of the Q[6], Q[6]-MA and MA.
(a) XPS spectra of the Q[6]-CQDs; (b–d) high resolution spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively.

(a) Molecular structure of Q[6]-MA-[CdCl4]2−; (b) Hydrogen-bonding interactions between Q[6], [CdCl4]2− and MA; (c) contact between the [CdCl4]2− and Q[6] ion–dipole; (d) The Q[6]-[CdCl4]2− interactions.

(a) 3D supramolecular network of Q[6]-MA-[CdCl4]2−; (b) One-dimensional supramolecular chain 1 of Q[6]-MA-[CdCl4]2−; (c) One-dimensional supramolecular chain 2 of Q[6]-MA-[CdCl4]2−; (d) Diagram of self-induced action of Q[6].

Fluorescence response of Q[6]-CQDs (400 mg/L;λmax = 405 nm) to metal ions (metal ions (aq.), 0.4 mM.): (a) emission spectra of each metal ion (aq.); (b) Photographs taken with a 365 nm UV camera of the Q[6]-CQDs solutions containing different metal ions.

(a-c) The fluorescence spectra of Q[6]-CQDs solution containing different concentrations of Fe3+ (0,20,40,60…260 μM), Ba2+ (0,100,200,300…1000 μM) and Al3+ (0,26.7,53.4,80.1…213 μM); (d-f) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity differentials of Q[6]-CQDs with the concentration of Fe3+, Ba2+ and Al3+. The concentration of Q[6]-CQDs was 400 mg/L, the excitation wavelength was 230 nm, and the solvent was ultrapure water.

Fluorescence response of Q[6]-CQDs (400 mg/L;λmax = 405 nm) to anions (anions (aq.), 0.33 mM.): (a) emission spectra of each anion (aq.); (b) Photographs taken with a 365 nm UV camera of the Q[6]-CQDs solutions containing different anions.

(a-b) The fluorescence spectra of Q[6]-CQDs solution containing different concentrations of I- (0,13.3,26.6,39.9,…147 μM) and ClO- (0,26.7,53.4,80.1,…347 μM); (c-d) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity differentials of Q[6]-CQDs with the concentration of I- and ClO-. The concentration of Q[6]-CQDs was 400 mg/L, the excitation wavelength was 230 nm, and the solvent was ultrapure water.

head.ce:title: A cucurbit[6]uril-based carbon dot for recognizing metal ions and anions in solutions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Molecular Medicine, School of Basic Medical Science, Guizhou Medical University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Molecular Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center for Molecular Medicine, School of Basic Medical Science, Guizhou Medical University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using cucurbit[6]uril (Q[6]) and mandelic acid (MA). The crystal structure of Q[6]-MA-[CdCl4]2- was determined by single crystal X-ray crystallography. The Q[6]-CQDs showed good water solubility and stable optical properties. Based on it, we used Q[6]-CQDs to construct a fluorescent probe for detecting and recognizing Fe3+, Ba2+, Al3+, I- and ClO-. The Fe3+ and I- could significantly reduce its fluorescence, while Al3+, Ba2+ and ClO- could enhance its fluorescence, and when Al3+ was added to Q[6]-CQDs, there was an obvious blue shift.

In this paper, fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using cucurbit[6]uril (Q[6]) and mandelic acid (MA). Compared with other carbon quantum dots, cucurbit[6]uril has the advantage that its original rigid macrocyclic skeleton was completely retained during the synthesis process. In addition, the performance of the Q[6]-CQDs were characterized by fluorescence and NMR spectroscopies, then the crystal structure of Q[6]-MA-[CdCl4]2- was determined by the single crystal X-ray crystallography. The Q[6]-CQDs showed good water solubility and stable optical property. Subsequently, using the obtained Q[6]-CQDs, a universal fluorescent probe for detecting and recognizing Fe3+, Ba2+, Al3+, I- and ClO- has been developed based on macrocyclic chemistry. Under ideal conditions, the detection limits were calculated to be 3.89 × 10-6 M, 2.58 × 10-5 M, 1.42 × 10-5 M, 6.84 × 10-6 M and 1.50 × 10-5 M.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanomaterials composed of carbon elements with a size of less than 10 nm [1], with excellent optical [2–4], electronic and catalytic properties [5–10]. They were discovered by Xu and others in 2004 when they purified single wall carbon nanotube by electrophoresis assay [11]. They are widely used in the fields of biomarkers [12–14], fluorescence sensing [15–17], photochemical catalysis and solar cells [18–21]. The preparation methods of CQDs mainly include top-down method and bottom-up method [22–24]. The top-down method decomposes macromolecules or graphene-like materials into small molecules or nanosheets by high-energy ball milling, laser ablation or arc discharge, while the bottom-up method polymerizes small molecules or monomers into CQDs by hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave radiation or pyrolysis methods. The surface functional groups of CQDs have an important influence on their optical property, which can be regulated by post-treatment or doping [22]. Supramolecular chemistry is a discipline of intermolecular self-assembly based on non-covalent forces. Non-covalent forces mainly involve hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, metal coordination, electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking. In recent years, the constructions and applications based on supramolecular chemistry were very widely [25–28]. Moreover, the supramolecular chemistry also has provided a novel way and method for the preparation of CQDs [29–32]. The morphology, size and fluorescence properties of CQDs can be controlled by supramolecular assembly techniques. For example, Zhang and others realized matrix-free room temperature phosphorescence based on carbon dots supramolecular chemistry [33]. Zheng’s team prepared stable fluorescent nanoparticles for noninvasive long-term bioimaging by supramolecular assembly of carbon dots and hydrophobic AIEgen [34]. Zhang and his partners prepared a supramolecular polymer hydrogel composed of poly(vinyl alcohol)/ chitosan carbon dots, which can be further used in biomedical research [35].

Cucurbit[n]urils (Q[n]s), as a novel class of host compounds, are cyclic macromolecules composed of glycoluril units bridged by methylene groups, which have excellent thermal and chemical stability [36–41]. Its hydrophobic cavity can form host–guest inclusion complexes with various organic molecules, and its port carbonyl oxygen can coordinate with metal ions, also its electropositive external surface can be connected to small organic molecules through the outer surface interaction. However, Q[n]s also has the disadvantages that its poor solubility in pure water, insolubility in most organic solvents, difficulty in functional modification, and no absorption in the visible region. Research has shown that the synthesis of CQDs with Q[n]s has excellent water solubility and easy to be functionalized, which can greatly improve the above shortcomings [42–44]. Therefore, we introduce Q[n]s to combine with carbon quantum dots to regulate the solubility, optical properties and self-assembly behavior of carbon quantum dots and cucurbit[n]urils.

In recent years, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have been primarily utilized as fluorescent probes due to their exceptional optical properties. For instance, Azad H. Alshatteri and his team employed yellow fluorescence-emitting carbon dots to intelligently detect bilirubin via mobile phones [45]. Similarly, Q. Hassan and his colleagues used dual-emission polymer carbon dots for the selective detection of Fe3+ and Al3+ [46]. Khalid M. Omer group prepared nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots through solvothermal synthesis followed by microwave irradiation, which can selectively detect Fe3+ [47]. However, most of these fluorescent probes are selective, with a limited range of detection categories. This highlights the need for the development of a universal probe based on carbon quantum dot.

In this paper, carbon quantum dots (Q[6]-CQDs) based on Q[6] were synthesized by using mandelic acid (MA) which contains C and O, the Q[6] as potential nitrogen sources. The Q[6]-CQDs exhibit excellent water solubility and stable optical properties. Furthermore, the original rigid macrocyclic skeleton of Q[6] is fully preserved in the Q[6]-CQDs. This method not only significantly enhances the water solubility of cucurbit[n]urils but also introduces a novel preparation technique for cucurbit[n]urils in fluorescent probe applications. Based on this, we use this CQDs as a universal fluorescent probe for the detection of metal ions and anions. The structures of Q[6] and MA are shown in Fig. 1 .

All materials, reagents, instruments, and detailed experimental methods such as 1H NMR, Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Single crystal X-ray crystallography, fluorescence spectrum and preparation of probe experiments were presented in the “Supplementary Information”.

Q[6]-CQDs were synthesized by traditional hydrothermal method from pure MA and Q[6] (see the “Supplementary Information” for details). In the UV/vis spectra, the maximum absorption peaks of Q[6]-CQDs in aqueous solution at 252 nm (Fig. 2 a). The absorption peaks at 252 nm are attributed to the π → π * transitions of the C = C bonds. The solution of Q[6]-CQDs emitted a blue fluorescence (λmax = 405 nm) (Fig. 2b), while the UV–vis absorption behavior was not observed in the Q[6] aqueous solution. Subsequently, in order to explore the stability of Q[6]-CQDs in solutions with different pH, we explored the effect of different pH on the fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs (Fig. 2c). It was found that the fluorescence emission intensity of Q[6]-CQDs did not change significantly with the increase of pH from 2 to 10. However, when the pH increased to 11 and 12, the fluorescence emission intensity increased. This indicates that Q[6]-CQDs are very sensitive in strong alkaline environment, while the fluorescence emission of Q[6]-CQDs will not be affected in normal pure water solvent, which is similar to CQDs containing hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties [48].

At the same time, we used 1H NMR to investigate the interaction between MA and Q[6] before the hydrothermal reaction, as shown in Fig. S1. After the addition of Q[6], Ha, Hb, Hc and He the proton peaks corresponding to MA were all shifted downfield. Furthermore, it can also be confirmed by crystal structure, the crystal structure shows that MA and Q[6] are connected by outer surface interaction. Then we carried out a series of researchs on Q[6]-CQDs. Importantly, the original proton peaks of Q[6] were observed in the 1H NMR spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs (Fig. S2), indicating that the rigid macrocyclic skeleton of Q[6] was remained during the hydrothermal reaction.

In addition, to better understand the difference between the groups Q[6]-MA and Q[6]-CQDs, the surface functional groups of Q[6]-MA and Q[6]-CQDs were determined by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2e and Table S1). The strong absorption peaks at 1234 cm−1 and 1737 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C = O, respectively [49]. The peaks at 1737 cm−1 and 1479 cm−1 are in accord with the C = O stretching and N–H bending [50]. However, FTIR spectrum also confirmed that Q[6]-CQDs contains functional groups of –OH (3210 cm−1), C = O (1583 cm−1), C-N (1234 cm−1), C-O and C-O-C (1000 ∼ 1200 cm−1). Besides, new absorption bands belonging to the stretching vibration of C–H (3006 cm−1) were observed in Q[6]-CQDs (Fig. 2d) [42,43]. Moreover, the morphology of the synthesizedQ[6]-CQDs was recorded by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. S3) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S3, the synthesized carbon dots are spherical and the crystal plane spacing is about 0.41 nm. The Raman spectrum analysis also proved the formation of carbon dots (Fig. S5).

Also, in order to understand the chemical composition of Q[6]-CQDs, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to determine it. Fig. 3 a clearly shows the peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s, which were located at 286.88, 399.48 and 531.28 eV, respectively, indicating that Q[6]-CQDs are mainly composed of C, N and O elements. The elemental contents of Q[6]-CQDs was found to be C, 45.05 %, N, 28.36 % and O, 26.59 %, respectively. The higher content of C and O confirmed that Q[6]-CQDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. Fig. 3b shows the fine spectrum of C1s, the absorptions at 284.28, 286.88 and 288.58 eV indicate the existence of C–C/C = C, C-N and C = O bonds in Q[6]-CQDs, respectively. In the N1s spectra (Fig. 3c), the absorptions at 399.48 and 400.88 eV can be attributed to the C-N and N–H bonds, and in the O1s spectra (Fig. 3d), the absorptions at 531.18 and 532.38 eV correspond to the C = O and C-OH bonds. The presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as C = C, C = O, C-N and C-OH enhances the water solubility and stability of Q[6]-CQDs, thus making the Q[6]-CQDs highly dispersed and stable in aqueous solution [42,43]. The above analysis verified that Q[6] -CQDs were successfully synthesized.

The x-ray crystal structure analysis provided unequivocal proof for the outer surface interaction mode between Q[6] and MA. We used Q[6] and MA as raw materials, then added CdCl2 which can form [CdCl4]2- as a structural inducer in 6 M HCl solution, whereupon, we prepared a supramolecular self-assembly composed of Q[6]/MA and [CdCl4]2- (complex 1).

Fig. 4 a depicts Q[6]-MA crystals in the centric space group P-1 with four Q[6] molecules, one guest molecule mandelic acid, three [CdCl4]2- ions and three bound water. The crystal structure shows that the main driving force between the host and guest is the outer surface interaction. Table S2 provides more specific crystal data. There are four Q[6] molecules in each basic unit, but only one Q[6] molecule interacts with MA. Fig. 4b shows that the hydroxyl oxygen (O10) of MA and the hydrogen (C03B-H03C) on the bridged methylene of Q[6] form a hydrogen bond. The distance between C03B-H03C…O10 is 2.860 Å. One Q[6] can interact with two MA molecules, and the MA molecules are symmetrical on both sides of Q[6] molecule. Interestingly, the guest MA is not directly connected to two Q[6] molecules, but is connected to Q[6] through hydrogen bonds on one side, and on the other side through hydrogen bonds (C8-H8…Cl08) formed by hydrogen (C8-H8) on the benzene of MA molecule and [CdCl4]2-, then the [CdCl4]2- is connected to Q[6] through ion–dipole interaction to form a one-dimensional supramolecular long chain as shown in Fig. 5 c. The distance between C8-H8…Cl08 is 2.795 Å. Fig. 4c and 4d demonstrate the outer surface interaction between Q[6] and [CdCl4]2−.

More novel assembly modes are found when the focus turns to the whole crystal structure. Through the layered structure of the crystal, it can be found that the Q[6]-MA crystal not only forms a one-dimensional supramolecular long chain 1 (Fig. 5c), but also forms another one-dimensional supramolecular chain 2 without the guest MA, which is only connected through the hydrogen bonds formed by the carbonyl oxygen at the Q[6] port and the nitrogen or hydrogen on the waist (Fig. 5b). [CdCl4]2- is linked to Q[6] directly or coordinated with water molecules, then the water molecules are combined with the carbonyl oxygen atoms of Q[6] by hydrogen bonds, so that the two supramolecular chains are combined to form a two-dimensional layered structure, even a three-dimensional space structure (Fig. 5a). In particular, after the combination of two supramolecular chains, it can be clearly seen that there are holes surrounded by four Q[6] molecules in the supramolecular self-assembly (Fig. 5d). Based on these holes, the supramolecular self-assembly of Q[6]-MA may play a role in other applications in the future.

An ideal fluorescent probe should have high selectivity to the analyte. Based on the stable optical properties of Q[6]-CQDs, we investigated the fluorescence response of Q[6]-CQDs to different metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs at 405 nm changed after adding Fe3+, Ba2+ and Al3+ to the aqueous solution of Q[6]-CQDs. After adding 6 μL of 0.2 M Fe3+ solution to the Q[6]-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 405 nm strongly reduced. Secondly, after adding Ba2+ solution to the Q[6]-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 405 nm increased strongly. Then, when Al3+ was added to the Q[6]-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the solution increased and accompanied by an obvious blue shift. Moreover, the other 19 metal ions had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs. It indicates that the Q[6]-CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+, Ba2+ and Al3+.

In order to further understand the recognition ability of Q[6]-CQDs toward Fe3+, Ba2+ and Al3+, luminescence titration experiments were performed. It can be seen from Fig. 7 a that the fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs gradually decreased with the addition of Fe3+. There was a good linear relationship between the differentials of fluorescence intensity observed at 405 nm with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 20 ∼ 100 μM (Fig. 7d), then the LOD was calculated to be 3.89 × 10-6 M. Fig. 7b shows that the fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs gradually increased with the addition of Ba2+. The fluorescence intensity differentials had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Ba2+ in the range of 100 ∼ 500 μM (Fig. 7e), and the LOD was 2.58 × 10-5 M. Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. 7c, with the continuous addition of Al3+, the fluorescence spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs showed an obvious blue shift and the fluorescence intensity increased. The differentials of fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Al3+ in the range of 53.4 ∼ 186.9 μM (Fig. 7f). Finally, the LOD was calculated to be 1.42 × 10-5 M.

Subsequently, we further investigated the potential mechanism of Fe3+ reducing the fluorescence of Q[6]-CQDs in aqueous solution by UV/vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S6a, the UV–vis absorption bands of Fe3+ (291–412 nm) and the fluorescence emission spectra of Q[6]-CQDs almost did not overlap, so the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer from the light emitted by the Q[6]-CQDs to Fe3+ was excluded. However, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs had a large overlap with the UV–vis absorption spectrum (262–388 nm) of Fe3+.Therefore, the energy which used to excite Q[6]-CQDs will be competitively intercepted by Fe3+, thus the excitation of Q[6]-CQDs was hindered, which may be the reason of the fluorescence of Q[6]-CQDs solution can be quenched by the Fe3+ [51,52]. Moreover, the potential mechanism of Al3+ and Ba2+ enhancing the fluorescence of Q[6]-CQDs was also investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S7a and Fig. S7b, after adding Al3+ and Ba2+ to Q[6]-CQDs, some new bonds that were not present before in Q[6]-CQDs appeared in the range of 3703 cm−1 to 2956 cm−1. This can be attributed to complexation among functional groups on the Q[6]-CQDs and Al3+, Ba2+ [4]. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. S8, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Al3+ exhibits partial overlap with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs. This suggests that fluorescence resonance energy transfer may also influence the interaction between Q[6]-CQDs and Al3+ [43,53]. In Fig. S7b, Upon the addition of Ba2+ to the Q[6]-CQDs, the peaks at 1589 cm−1 underwent a red shift to 1631 cm-1, and a new peak was observed at 2021 cm−1. This indicates the presence of coordination between Ba2+ and the carbonyl oxygen atoms. This coordination obstructs the photoinduced electron transfer, resulting in enhanced fluorescence [54].

In order to further expand the application of Q[6]-CQDs, the fluorescence response of Q[6]-CQDs to a series of common anions was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 a, when the I- was added to the aqueous solution of Q[6]-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity at 405 nm decreased. On the contrary, when the ClO- was added to the aqueous solution of Q[6]-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity increased, and the other 14 anions did not affect the fluorescence intensity of Q-CQDs. This indicates that the Q[6]-CQDs have the potential to recognize I- and ClO-.

For the purpose of understanding the recognition ability of Q[6]-CQDs to I- and ClO-, luminescence titration experiments were performed, too. It can be observed from Fig. 9 a that with the continuous addition of I-, the fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs gradually decreased. The differentials of the fluorescence intensity observed at 405 nm showed a good linear relationship with I- in the range of 26.6～93.1 μM (Fig. 9c), and the LOD was calculated to be 6.84 × 10-6 M. Fig. 9b shows that the fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs gradually increased with the continuous addition of ClO-. The differentials of fluorescence intensity at 405 nm had a good linear relationship with the concentration of ClO- in the range of 186.9 ∼ 320.4 μM (Fig. 9d), and the LOD was 1.50 × 10-5 M.

Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of I- inducing Q[6]-CQDs was investigated by UV/vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S9a, there was no obvious overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of I- and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs, indicating that the energy used to excite the Q[6]-CQDs was not absorbed by I-. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of Q[6]-CQDs was not due to the competitive absorption mechanism of I- and Q[6]-CQDs. However, the UV–vis absorption peaks of Q[6]-CQDs at 252 nm disappeared with the addition of the I- to the Q[6]-CQDs (Fig. S9b). This suggests the formation of stable excited complexes between Q[6]-CQDs and I-. The addition of I- may promote π-π stacking between chromophores, resulting in a larger non-radiative transition, which induced the fluorescence quenching of Q[6]-CQDs [55]. Similarly, there was no obvious overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of ClO- and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs (Fig. S10a), but the UV–vis absorption peaks of Q[6]-CQDs at 252 nm increased after the addition of ClO- (Fig. S10b), which may be due to the strong oxidation ability of ClO-, and can react with Q[6]-CQDs to increase the fluorescence intensity [56].

In this paper, carbon quantum dots (Q[6]-CQDs) based on Q[6] and mandelic acid were synthesized by a hydrothermal method, which was characterized by FTIR, XPS, 1H NMR and other techniques. The Q[6]-CQDs showed good water solubility and stable optical properties. We explored the effect of different pH on it, and it was most favorable for its fluorescence intensity in a strong alkaline environment. The Q[6]-CQDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+, Al3+, Ba2+, I- and ClO- in aqueous solution. The Fe3+ and I- could significantly reduce its fluorescence, while Al3+, Ba2+ and ClO- could enhance its fluorescence, and when Al3+ was added to Q[6]-CQDs, there was an obvious blue shift. Under ideal conditions, the detection limits of Fe3+, Ba2+, Al3+, I- and ClO- were calculated to be 3.89 × 10-6 M, 2.58 × 10-5 M, 1.42 × 10-5 M, 6.84 × 10-6 M and 1.50 × 10-5 M. This study presents a universal probe capable of detecting both metal ions and anions in solutions. It also introduces a new approach to designing cucurbit[n]urils-based sensors for a variety of analytes.

XingYue Yuan: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. ZhiChao Yu: Data curation. JianHang Hu: Methodology. Bo Xiao: Writing – review & editing. TingTing Zhang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Kui Li: Writing – review & editing. Chang Chen: Writing – review & editing. Zhu Tao: Writing – review & editing. Xin Xiao: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Fund of Guizhou Province (ZK [2023] General 040) and Young scientific and technological talents of Guizhou Provincial Department of Education (KY[2021]165).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123632.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Mater. Chem. Fron.

Mater. Today Adv.
